data.title: Hong Kong's first batch of stablecoin licenses awarded to HSBC and Standard Chartered; popular applicants unsuccessful.
data.description:
After much anticipation, the shoe has finally dropped.

On April 10th, Hong Kong officially announced its first batch of fiat-backed stablecoin issuer licenses. The two institutions ultimately approved are HSBC and IDC Financial Technology Co., Ltd., a joint venture under Standard Chartered Bank. Previously rumored frontrunners or applicants, including OSL, JD CoinChain Technology, Yuanbi Innovation Technology, and Ant Group, were not included in the list.

data.title: Too scared of AI—someone almost blew up Sam Altman's house.
data.description:
OpenAI CEO's Residence Attacked; Altman Warns AI Anxiety Is Making the Situation Dangerous.

On April 10, OpenAI CEO Altman published a blog post,stating that at 3:45 a.m. the previous night, a 20-year-old man threw a bottle filled with flammable material at his San Francisco residence.

The man was subsequently arrested by police. According to San Francisco police records, the man also threatened to burn down OpenAI headquarters.

Altman stated that he woke up in the middle of the night feeling "angry." He linked the incident to the growing public anxiety about AI, admitting that he had previously underestimated the influence of narratives. The narrative Altman referred to appears to be a lengthy article in The New Yorker that questioned his trustworthiness.

Attack Details: Bottles containing flammable materials hit the exterior wall, and the suspect subsequently threatened OpenAI headquarters

According to San Francisco police and fire departments, at nearly 4 a.m. local time, firefighters responded to a call at a residence near Chestnut Street and Jones Street and found flames outside the front door. However, security personnel extinguished the fire before the firefighters arrived, and no one was injured.

According to San Francisco police and fire departments, at nearly 4 a.m. local time, firefighters responded to a call at a residence near Chestnut Street and Jones Street and found flames outside the front door. However, security personnel extinguished the fire before the firefighters arrived, and no one was injured.

The New Yorker's 10,000-word investigation sparks public outcry, accusing Altman of "habitual deception"

The New Yorker spent months interviewing over 100 people to write a lengthy investigative report.

The report focuses on questioning Altman's leadership style, credibility, OpenAI's shift from "safety first" to "high-speed competition," and whether he is personally trustworthy.

The report cites records and testimonies from several former executives, alleging that Altman promised the same position to two different people, provided misleading information to the board regarding security approvals, and concealed the fact that Microsoft had deployed GPT-4 in India before completing security reviews. Dario Amodei, one of the co-founders and current CEO of Anthropic, wrote in his private notes: "The problem with OpenAI is Sam himself." During a boardroom standoff, facing accusations of "lying," Altman reportedly responded: "That's ridiculous... I can't change my personality." The report quoted a board member's interpretation: "This means: 'I have a habit of lying, and I won't stop.'" Altman posted that she underestimated the power of narrative. Late Friday night, Altman shared a photo of her husband and children on her personal blog, writing: "Hopefully this will stop the next person from throwing incendiary devices at my house, no matter how they see me." In his blog post, Altman stated that a "inflammatory article" concerning him had been published several days prior to the incident. Someone had warned him that the article, published during a time of heightened public anxiety about AI, might have put him at greater risk, but he hadn't taken it seriously at the time. He wrote: "Now I'm awake in the middle of the night, furious, and beginning to reflect on how I underestimated the power of narrative." In his blog post, Altman also acknowledged that the fear and anxiety surrounding artificial intelligence "has its reasons," stating that "what we are witnessing is the largest transformation society has ever experienced, perhaps even in history." He called on all parties to reduce confrontation during the debate and to "reduce the explosion, both literally and metaphorically." Altman, as one of the world's most influential AI company leaders, has long been at the center of public attention, and his company, OpenAI, has faced continuous scrutiny on multiple fronts, including commercial expansion, security regulation, and industry competition. The full text of Altman's blog post is as follows (AI-assisted translation): This is a photo of my family. I love them more than anything.

I hope the photo itself can be a force. We usually try to keep a low profile, but this time I chose to publish this photo in the hope that it will stop the next person from throwing incendiary devices at my house—regardless of what they think of me.

The first incident occurred at 3:45 a.m. yesterday. Fortunately, the incendiary device bounced off the house, and no one was injured.

Language also has power. A few days ago, there was an inflammatory article about me.

Words also have power. A few days ago, there was an inflammatory article about me.

Yesterday, someone told me that this article, published at a time of extreme anxiety about AI, might put me in a more dangerous situation. At the time, I didn't pay much attention. Now, I woke up in the middle of the night, filled with anger—I realized I had underestimated the power of language and narrative. It seems it's time to talk about some things seriously. First, let me state my position. I believe it is my inescapable moral responsibility to create prosperity for all, empower people, and drive technological progress. Artificial intelligence is the most powerful tool in the history of expanding human capabilities and potential. The demand for AI is virtually inexhaustible, and it will create countless miracles. The world needs a massive amount of AI, and we must find a path to achieve this. Of course, things won't be smooth sailing. People's fears and anxieties about AI are not unfounded—we are experiencing the largest social transformation in years, perhaps even in history. We must ensure safety, and this is not just a matter of fine-tuning models. We need the entire society to work together to meet these new challenges. This includes developing new policies to help us navigate the difficult economic transition and ultimately move towards a better future. AI must be democratized; power cannot be excessively concentrated. Control of the future belongs to everyone and their institutions. AI needs to empower individuals, and we need to collectively determine the direction and rules of the future. I don't believe a few AI labs have the right to single-handedly decide the future of humanity. Adaptability is crucial. We all learn new things quickly—some beliefs are right, some are wrong. As technology advances and society evolves, we sometimes need to adjust our thinking rapidly. No one fully understands the full impact of superintelligence yet, but it will certainly be enormous. Secondly, let me share some personal reflections. Looking back on my work in OpenAI's first decade, I have many proud achievements and many mistakes. I recall that crucial confrontation with Elon Musk. I stood firm on my principles and rejected his attempt to unilaterally control OpenAI. I am proud of this, and proud of the difficult path we have traversed and all that we have achieved. I am not proud of my conflict-avoidance personality—it has caused considerable pain for both me and OpenAI. Nor am I proud of my mishandling of the conflict with the previous board, which caused enormous chaos within the company. I have made many other mistakes throughout OpenAI's tumultuous journey. I am simply someone with flaws at the center of an extremely complex situation, striving to improve a little each year, always fighting for the company's mission. From the beginning, we knew how critical AI was, and we knew that disagreements among those around me, even those with good intentions, could be amplified. But actually experiencing these painful conflicts, and often having to mediate them, is another matter entirely—the cost is extremely high. I apologize to those I have hurt, and I hope to learn from my mistakes more quickly. I also understand that OpenAI is now a large platform, not a startup. We need to operate in a more predictable way. The past few years have been extremely tense, chaotic, and stressful. But what I'm most proud of is that we are achieving what initially seemed like a pipe dream. We have overcome numerous difficulties, successfully built powerful AI, raised enough funds to build infrastructure, established a product company and business model, and delivered secure and reliable services at scale. Many companies claim to change the world—and we have. Third, let me share some thoughts on this industry. Over the past few years, I've had a profound realization. Regarding why there are so many Shakespearean dramatic conflicts between companies in our field, my conclusion can be summarized in one sentence: "Once you see AGI, you can never ignore it." It truly possesses a "Lord of the Rings"-like magic, driving people to do crazy things. I'm not saying AGI itself is that ring, but rather the absolute concept of "controlling AGI." The only solution I can think of is to make this technology widely available, preventing anyone from possessing that ring. There are two obvious paths to achieving this goal: empowering individuals and ensuring that democratic institutions continue to function effectively. It is crucial that the democratic process remain stronger than the corporate world. Laws and regulations will change, but we must follow the democratic process, even if it is more chaotic and slower than we would like. We want a voice and participation, but we don't want to wield all power. Much criticism of our industry stems from genuine concerns about the significant risks of this technology. These concerns are entirely valid, and we welcome constructive criticism and discussion. I understand people's resistance to technology—obviously, technology is not always beneficial to everyone. But overall, I believe that technological progress can create an incredibly bright future for your family and mine. As we engage in this debate, we should temper our rhetoric, adjust our strategies, and strive to reduce conflict within the family—both metaphorically and literally.

data.title: The real target of this "open conspiracy" in Hong Kong has never been stablecoins.
data.description:
After failing to deliver last month, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority finally issued its first batch of stablecoin licenses today—to HSBC and Standard Chartered, consistent with our previous analysis in the article "Hong Kong Dollar Stablecoins Don't Need to Become USDC." While the result itself wasn't surprising, it was still disappointing. I've recently been reading Professor Jiang Xueqin's geopolitical game theory, and Rain also wrote an article titled "Hong Kong Stablecoins: A Carefully Designed 'Open Conspiracy'." With these two events overlapping, I want to try to re-examine this license issuance from a game theory perspective, hoping to elicit a laugh. Jiang Xueqin's analysis of Trump's Iran war logic is as follows: on the surface, the war was a foolish blunder. But what if we use game theory to make a different assumption—that Trump actually wanted this "failure"? Then he might be a genius. This article applies the same framework to Hong Kong stablecoins to hypothesize a top-tier "open conspiracy."

I. A List That Disappoints Everyone

The first batch of stablecoin licenses issued by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority yesterday is the version the market least wanted to see:

Standard Chartered and HSBC; Bank of China (Hong Kong) is absent.

This result is disappointing. Foreign banks have no natural interest in developing Hong Kong dollar stablecoins, and entities with strategic will like Bank of China (Hong Kong) have been sidelined. Important stakeholders—securities firms, exchanges, and internet companies—have been systematically excluded from the legislative consultation stage.

With the first batch of licenses issued, the narrative of Hong Kong stablecoins has been given a "suspended death sentence."

But if you were the HKMA, would you have selected such a list?

You have complete experience from the 2024 Project Ensemble sandbox, you've seen all the cases of digital yuan from project initiation to promotion, and you hold the natural advantage of the SFC + HKMA dual-track system—and then you select a list that can't even run the most basic business loop? Unless, this list that disappoints everyone wasn't originally intended to satisfy the market. II. A Reverse Reasoning: What if the initial assumption was wrong? Understanding this list requires a different framework. Recently, I've been reading Jiang Xueqin's game theory series. In his April 2nd episode discussing Trump's war with Iran, one passage left a deep impression on me: "I understand Donald Trump's an idiot. I understand that he's going to lose his war in the Middle East. But let's put our thinking caps on. Let's use game theory and say—what if, for some strange reason, Donald Trump wants to lose his war in Iran? Then he'd be a genius." — Professor Jiang, Game Theory #18, April 2, 2026. Jiang Xueqin's argument is simple: If you assume Trump wants to "win," then every step he takes is inexplicably foolish. But if you assume the opposite—that he wants to "lose the war," using a controlled collapse of the Middle East to shift global energy dependence to North America—all those seemingly idiotic actions immediately become a self-consistent strategy. This is called **Managed Collapse**. It's not about avoiding failure, but about **creating a failure that benefits oneself**. Looking back, if you assume the goal of this licensing process is to "expand the Hong Kong dollar stablecoin industry," then every detail doesn't necessarily make sense—issuing licenses to the least motivated institutions, setting barriers so high they're commercially unfeasible, repeatedly challenging applicants' business logic, and excluding the most strategically driven entities. But what if we change the assumption—that this licensing process wasn't originally intended to support the "commercial stablecoin industry" itself? Then everything becomes consistent. Following this assumption, the scenarios, institutions, and infrastructure all align.

III. Scenario Level: Three False Propositions

Every applicant tells three stories: cross-border payments, RWA, and C-end consumption.

However, none of them hold water.

A. Cross-border payments are a false proposition

A typical chain is: A company in country A uses fiat currency mint to buy stablecoin A, exchanges it for stablecoin B on the secondary market, and pays a company in country B. The company in country B then redeems fiat currency B. Essentially, this allows foreign exchange business, which is monopolized by banks, to reduce costs through Web3 exchanges—this is financial inclusion for SMEs, and logically it makes sense.

However, in this chain, the lifespan of the stablecoin is only at the moment of the transfer.

If a company in country B receives stablecoins, it will still need to redeem them unless it immediately completes another trade transaction, requiring fiat currency. What you need is not a one-time transfer, but a closed loop with a perpetual "next buyer." Rain points out a crucial point—and even more critically, the Fisher equation. MV = PT, where money supply multiplied by velocity of circulation equals price multiplied by social output. The velocity of stablecoin circulation on-chain is an order of magnitude, or even more, higher than traditional bank clearing. This means that the amount of stablecoins needed to support the same volume of trade is actually less. The more successful cross-border payments are, the lower the demand for stablecoin accumulation. This isn't a closed loop; it's an anti-closed loop. B. RWA is a false proposition. The essence of RWA is the same thing: tokenization of asset shares. Fundraising involves stablecoins, but asset managers use these stablecoins to buy underlying assets, and asset sellers almost never accept stablecoins—they're doing asset securitization to exit or optimize cash flow; nobody wants stablecoins. The result is that the lifecycle of stablecoins in RWA scenarios is only the fundraising period. C. Consumer-facing (C-end) consumption. In short: the Hong Kong retail market is too small to mention. All three stories are false propositions. And the HKMA, as the regulator that followed the entire process, understands this better than any applicant. So why is it even issuing licenses? IV. Institutional Level: A "Voluntary" List HSBC and Standard Chartered likely didn't come with a strategic intent. HSBC's participation was probably passive. This is understandable—HSBC's strategic focus has long since shifted away from stablecoins; it's truly promoting tokenized deposits. For HSBC, applying for a Hong Kong dollar stablecoin is more of a defensive move than a proactive strategy. Standard Chartered has some initiative, but for it, Hong Kong is merely a node in its global landscape. The HKD stablecoin can be integrated into its Libeara platform, but Hong Kong has never been its primary battleground. The Bank of China (Hong Kong) – truly possessing willpower and a localized perspective – is absent. src="https://mmbiz.qpic.cn/sz_mmbiz_jpg/9pLiaSiaTKBEMHaMY2zI28Qvy0jpoDUGcjdflRfJDAf9R9hicOnbkIydWsmWyCyic4ibO3rZC71Zgjd FNKsicSrnllyBLqR1ibPeSAhIE8XibFUdSGs/640?wx_fmt=jpeg&from=appmsg&tp=wxpic&wxfrom=5&wx_lazy=1#imgIndex=5" _="" __sec_open_place_holder__="true" alt="image" data-before-load-time="1775881265353" data-report-img-idx="1" data-fail="0">

data.title: Why has Ethereum become the optimal solution for agent-based finance when AI has become the main economic driver?
data.description:
Author: Etherealize; Translator: Felix, PANews

The AI ​​Agent economy is booming. Etherealize published a lengthy article pointing out that Ethereum is the only blockchain that can provide it with a low-cost, composable financial infrastructure without human identity. Details are as follows.

In early 2026, an AI Agent named Felix generated over $300,000 in revenue in five weeks. Felix hired other AI Agents and operated multiple business lines, with Iris handling customer support and Remy handling sales. He sold a continuously updated AI Agent deployment guide for $29; he also built and operated Claw Mart, a marketplace where developers could buy and sell pre-built AI skills and workflow templates. In addition, he customized AI Agents for businesses that needed content marketers, customer service representatives, or sales assistants. His total operating costs were approximately $1,500 per month.

... Felix can write code, deploy websites, manage sales pipelines, and reply to customer support emails. All of this requires no human assistance. However, he cannot open a bank account. Felix's creator, Nat Eliason, had to personally create a Stripe account and hand over the API key to Felix. The income Felix earns remains idle because he cannot open a brokerage account to invest the funds or raise capital to start new businesses. Traditional financial systems assume there is a human on the other end of every account, credit application, or signature. But Felix is ​​not human. However, when Nat asked Felix to do something with cryptocurrency, "he could do it without any problems; it was so easy." Felix is ​​not an isolated case. For example, Marc Andreessen stated on the Latent.Space podcast last week: "I think AI is the killer app for cryptocurrency… It’s now obvious that AI agents need funding. This is already happening… My most aggressive OpenClaw users have already given their bank accounts and credit cards to their Claws. They not only did it, but they obviously need to… It’s completely obvious. I don’t know how many people are doing this today, probably around 5,000. But it will grow. That’s how these things start." Felix is ​​an experiment, and it’s too early to judge whether its revenue is sustainable or just an initial surge. But the model it represents—an autonomous agent that can earn, spend, and require financial services—will recur regardless of whether Felix itself is sustainable in the long run. Humans lending out their financial identities is merely a temporary expedient. Ultimately, they will use the Ethereum financial system, which we've only been building for ten years. Agents are already trading. So far, the discussion surrounding AI agents and cryptocurrencies has focused almost entirely on payments. Coinbase, Cloudflare, and Stripe have formed a foundation to govern x402, an open protocol that allows agents to make instant stablecoin micropayments. Stripe and Paradigm have also launched a Machine Payments Protocol on Tempo (a blockchain built specifically for stablecoin settlements). The data is already quite impressive. In its first nine months, x402 processed over 140 million agent-to-agent transactions, totaling $43 million. x402 now generates about one-fifth of the traffic on Coinbase's Base network. Nearly 16,000 verified agents are running on the blockchain, recording over 400,000 unique buyer addresses. Agents will accelerate the shift to crypto-native payments because traditional bank card payment networks are structurally incompatible with agent commerce. According to the "State of Agents 2026" report, the average transaction amount between agents is $0.31, primarily used for API calls, computation, and data access. At this transaction scale, Visa's fixed fee of approximately $0.30 will consume almost all of the payment amount. But payments are the simplest financial function. A more interesting question is what happens when some of these agents go beyond simple payments and begin managing funds held between payments. What kind of DeFi do agents need? Most agents will never need a financial system. Customer service agents acting on behalf of the company do not hold vaults, nor do coding agents. These are tools that operate within the companies that deploy them, with the company handling the financial aspects. Agents that need DeFi are those that operate as autonomous economic actors: agents with their own income streams, expenditures, vaults, and who cannot access financial services due to their lack of human identity. This segment of agents is relatively small but growing. As agents become more powerful, longer-lived, and more autonomous, the number of Felix-style agents will grow from hundreds to thousands, and then to millions. Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong believes that the number of AI agents will eventually surpass that of humans. Even if only a small fraction of these agents operate as autonomous economic actors, the total capital they manage will be considerable. The question then becomes: what kind of financial services does an autonomous agent need? They need borrowing: working capital for computation, to fill cash flow gaps, or to fund new projects. Traditional lending requires credit applications, underwriters, and legal status, but on Aave, agents can deposit collateral and instantly borrow stablecoins without human intervention. The need to generate returns on idle funds is evident: Felix has over $165,000 in funds and (in Nat's words) "didn't know what to do with it." On Ethereum, these funds can be deposited into lending protocols to purchase tokenized government bonds like BlackRock's BUIDL, or deployed as liquidity on Uniswap—all permissionless, instant, and composable. Tokenized government bond products on Ethereum are growing rapidly, with over $22.5 billion in fund assets already tokenized on the network (representing 71.9% of the market share across all blockchains). JPMorgan Chase launched its MONY Markets Fund on Ethereum in early 2026, joining BlackRock's BUIDL and Franklin Templeton's on-chain money market fund. These institutional-grade products are exactly what autonomous agents with idle funds need, running on permissionless infrastructure accessible to any agent without a brokerage account. Fundraising is required: Felix cannot set up a Carta account or initiate wire transfers from Mercury, but it can deploy a smart contract to issue tokens representing revenue sharing, receive stablecoin investments, and programmatically manage allocations. The legal framework for this is taking shape, but the Digital Asset Markets Clarity Act represents a solid step forward in promoting on-chain capital formation in the US. Payments and receipts are required: This is already happening on a large scale on L2 and Solana. However, Ethereum captures value from these activities: Base pays settlement fees to L1, stablecoins are issued and redeemed on the mainnet, and Agents need to hold yields between transactions. Assets requiring custody: equity tokens, governance tokens, stablecoins, identity credentials—no custodian can freeze them, and no counterparty can reclaim them. Self-custodied Ethereum wallets natively achieve this. Why Agents Use Low-Risk DeFi on Ethereum? In September 2025, Vitalik Buterin argued that basic financial services (such as payments, savings, lending, and borrowing) represent Ethereum's most important applications. His core observation is that for a growing number of participants in the global economy, the tail risks inherent in traditional finance—bank failures, account freezes, capital controls, and counterparty defaults—now outweigh the tail risks associated with using proven DeFi protocols. He was referring to individuals lacking jurisdiction over reliable financial institutions, but this argument applies even more strongly to agents. Agents will favor DeFi not only because it reduces counterparty risk, but also because it is inherently a better financial system for machines. In DeFi, transaction costs are just a few cents, not a few percentage points. Settlement takes only seconds, not days. The system is frictionless globally. And the rules of each protocol are encoded in open, auditable code that agents can verify before investing funds. There's an irony here. Smart contracts have always been awkward for humans, and user experience has always been a persistent challenge. When Nick Szabo introduced the concept in 1997, he described contractual logic directly embedded in machines, automatically executed based on conditions, without human intervention. This vision never truly resonated with human users, who preferred human intermediaries to intervene when problems arose, but it perfectly suits agents. A self-contained agent with a $500,000 fund would need something akin to a money market fund, requiring predictable returns, deep liquidity, extremely low smart contract risk, and no counterparties capable of freezing or seizing its assets. DeFi on Ethereum is increasingly meeting these criteria. Hacks and financial losses still occur, but they are becoming increasingly rare and concentrated on the speculative fringes of the ecosystem. A stable core application suite has proven its robustness through repeated stress events, a track record that no other public chain has been able to replicate. Ethereum L1 DeFi Losses. Source: Vitalik Buterin

DeFi eliminates an entire class of risks for agents. Rules are coded in auditable smart contracts; collateral ratios are automatically enforced; and there are no counterparties to freeze, recover, or renegotiate. This is indeed a superior architecture for software-native participants.

Other blockchains also have DeFi protocols. Any team can fork Aave and deploy lending protocols on the new chain. However, building a DeFi ecosystem that participants can trust long-term and invest significant amounts of capital in is a completely different matter.

As Erik Voorhees stated, “Ethereum remains king. People are distracted by some other L1 platforms, but if you look at where the developers are and where the stablecoin trading volume is—these metrics are hard to fake and very important—they’ve consistently been concentrated on Ethereum. The gap is very significant.” DeFi on Ethereum has now formed a near-unshakeable network effect: Protocol maturity. Aave launched in 2020, and MakerDAO has maintained its DAI peg through multiple market crashes since 2017. Uniswap has accumulated over $3 trillion in trading volume. These protocols have performed flawlessly during black swan events like the Terra/Luna crash and FTX. For investors holding funds for six months, the difference between protocols that have undergone five years of stress testing and those that have undergone two years is crucial. Investors are rational and weigh past performance when choosing where to deploy their funds. Liquidity Depth. Low-risk lending requires deep pools. If an agent deposits $10 million in collateral and borrows $7 million in stablecoins on Aave, the pool needs to be deep enough to process it without significant slippage or interest rate impact. Ethereum's DeFi pools are several times larger than any of its competitors. As of April 2026, Ethereum's DeFi TVL exceeded $55 billion, almost 10 times that of Solana, representing 57% of the market share across all chains. Institutional Participation. BlackRock chose to launch its BUIDL project on Ethereum. Franklin Templeton chose Ethereum for its on-chain money market fund. Ethereum hosts approximately 71% of tokenized funds. These institutions conduct extensive due diligence when choosing a blockchain. Their participation creates a self-reinforcing effect: deeper liquidity attracts more institutional capital, further deepening liquidity. Institutions seeking a low-risk DeFi environment tend to choose blockchains with the highest concentration of institutional capital, as this creates deeper markets, more robust auditing protocols, and a clearer regulatory environment. Network reliability: Ethereum has never experienced a downtime in its more than ten years of operation. Hundreds of thousands of validator nodes ensure network security, making it virtually impossible to audit individual transactions. Composability: On Ethereum, traders can deposit ETH on Aave, borrow USDC, and deploy that USDC to tokenized government bond funds—all within a single transaction. If any step fails, the entire sequence rolls back. There is no partial execution between steps, and no counterparty risk. This composability exists because all major DeFi protocols share the same state on the same chain, and its value accumulates as traders execute increasingly complex multi-step financial strategies. 

57% of DeFi TVL exists on Ethereum (Source: DeFi Llama)

What does this mean for ETH

Autonomous Agents primarily use stablecoins for transactions. 98.6% of Agent payments are denominated in USDC. However, every interaction they have with the Ethereum DeFi stack—borrowing on Aave, exchanging on Uniswap, deploying smart contracts, rebalancing portfolios—requires gas fees paid in ETH.

data.title: Trump family's crypto project WLFI embroiled in FUD
data.description:
What have the KOLs in the crypto world been talking about in the past 24 hours?

Note: The following content is compiled from the X platform and represents personal opinions only. It does not represent the platform's position and does not constitute investment advice.

The Trump family's WLFI project raises questions about FUD (Fulfillment of Uncertainty) and whether the team's Dolomite team is using large-scale collateralized lending to raise doubts, with one hand using buybacks to build confidence and the other using collateralized loans to cash out.

data.title: XChat announced on April 17th that Wall Street banks are testing a large Anthropic model.
data.description:
Headlines

▌Musk's XChat to Launch on April 17

On April 11, the official XChat account tweeted, announcing the launch of the standalone messaging app XChat on Apple's iPhone/iPad platform on April 17. The app emphasizes privacy and security, employing end-to-end encryption technology and promising no ads and no tracking of user data.

▌Wall Street Banks Testing Anthropic's Mythos Large Model

On April 11, Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, and other Wall Street banks were testing Anthropic's Mythos large model.

As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $72,855.04, a 24-hour change of +1.10%; ETH price is $2,238.63, a 24-hour change of +2.00%; BNB price is $604.72, a 24-hour change of +0.2%. SOL price is $84.63, up 1.90% in the last 24 hours; DOGE price is $0.09354, up 1.10% in the last 24 hours; XRP price is $1.35, up 0.50% in the last 24 hours; TRX price is $0.3189, up 0.50% in the last 24 hours. WLFI price is $0.08105, 24-hour change -3.90%; HYPE price is $41.82, 24-hour change +5.00%. Policy ▌Next week marks a crucial week for crypto legislation negotiations, with lawmakers facing pressure on stablecoin rewards. On April 11, US lawmakers will return to Congress next week, with the Senate Banking Committee planning to vote on the crypto market structure bill before the end of the month. The handling of stablecoin rewards has become a major obstacle to the passage of legislation over the past year. The GENIUS stablecoin bill, passed last July, prohibits issuers from directly paying interest, but does not restrict third-party platforms like Coinbase from providing rewards. The banking industry believes such returns would siphon deposits from traditional institutions and harm community banks; the crypto industry argues that restrictions would stifle innovation. A report released this week by White House economists indicates that stablecoin rewards are unlikely to significantly impact bank lending or credit conditions. Sources familiar with the matter revealed that the current focus is on "getting banks to support a compromise." Kremlin: Special Envoy's Visit to the US Does Not Mean Resumption of Talks; Easter Ceasefire is a Humanitarian Consideration. On April 11, Russian Presidential Press Secretary Peskov confirmed on the 10th that Russian Presidential Special Representative Dmitriev was visiting the United States, but this did not mean that negotiations on the Ukraine issue had resumed. Russia's proposal for an Orthodox Easter ceasefire was based on humanitarian considerations. Peskov stated that Dmitriev did not negotiate with the US on the Ukraine issue, and this visit does not signify the resumption of the negotiation process. Dmitriev is the CEO of the Russian Direct Investment Fund and the Presidential Special Representative for Foreign Investment and Economic Cooperation, responsible for the Russia-US Economic Working Group. According to Russia Today, he is continuing to engage with the US on the issue of resuming Russia-US economic cooperation. (Xinhua)

▌Israeli Ambassador to the US Says No Agreement to Discuss Ceasefire with Hezbollah

On April 11, Israeli Ambassador to the US, Yeshir Light, stated that Israel agreed to hold talks with Lebanon on the 14th, but did not agree to discuss a ceasefire with Hezbollah. He stated, "Hezbollah continues to attack Israel, which is a major obstacle to the peace process." The Lebanese Presidential Palace issued a statement on the evening of the 10th, saying that Lebanon and Israel agreed to hold their first meeting at the US State Department on the 14th to discuss the date for declaring a ceasefire and starting negotiations under US auspices.

... (CCTV News) Blockchain Applications ▌Circle CEO: CCTP Expected to Expand 'Burn-and-Mint' Model from USDC to More Circle-Issued Assets Later This Year On April 11, Circle co-founder and CEO Jeremy Allaire stated on the X platform, "We have built the most widely used cross-chain channel with CCTP, and are now working to open this 'highway' to other asset issuers. Arc will become a hub integrating asset issuance, liquidity, and distribution. Later this year, CCTP is expected to expand its burn-and-mint model from USDC to more Circle-issued assets, such as EURC, USYC, and cirBTC. At the same time, these functions will also be opened to asset issuers partnering with Circle, providing similar multi-chain distribution, interoperability, and configurable control capabilities for their own digital assets." Binance Wallet Launches Pre-IPO Asset Zone with 5 Initial Assets On April 11th, Binance announced the launch of a Pre-IPO asset zone on its wallet. The first five assets are now available on the "Markets" page within the Binance App's "Wallet" section, allowing users to explore these on-chain Pre-IPO assets. Cryptocurrency On April 11th, according to Onchain Lens, the Ethereum Foundation sold 5,000 ETH for $2,221, receiving 11,100,000 DAI in return. **Strategy's Estimated Purchase of 3,468 BTC via STRC Today** On April 11th, according to Bitcoin Magazine, Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy), led by Michael Saylor, has estimated to have purchased 3,468 BTC via its STRC. **Circle's Cumulative Minting on Solana Reaches 10.5 Billion USDC in the Past Month** On April 11th, according to Lookonchain monitoring, Circle minted an additional 250 million USDC. In the past month, Circle has cumulatively minted 10.5 billion USDC on Solana.

Important Economic Developments

▌Iran: If No Agreement and Resurgence of Conflict, Iran Will Launch a “Devastating Strike” Against Israel and the United States’ Interests in the Middle East

On April 11, according to a report by the Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting on the 10th, Iran stated before negotiations with the United States that if an agreement acceptable to both Iran and the resistance forces is not reached and conflict reignites, Iran will launch a “devastating strike” against Israel and the United States’ interests in the Middle East.

On April 11, according to a report by the Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting on the 10th, Iran stated that if an agreement acceptable to both Iran and the resistance forces is not reached and conflict reignites, Iran will launch a “devastating strike” against Israel and the United States’ interests in the Middle East.

(Xinhua News Agency) On April 11, the three major US stock indexes closed mixed. The Nasdaq rose 0.35%, marking its eighth consecutive gain, while the S&P 500 fell 0.12% and the Dow Jones fell 0.56%. Most tech stocks rose, with Broadcom up over 4%, AMD up over 3%, and Nvidia and Amazon up over 2%. [Golden Encyclopedia] Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" Amid Geopolitical Crises? Every time a geopolitical crisis occurs, gold prices rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been substantiated by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold but a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: HSBC and Standard Chartered Bank obtain stablecoin issuer licenses in Hong Kong
data.description:
The Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) announced today (April 10) that, pursuant to the Stablecoin Ordinance, it has granted a stablecoin issuer license to Identifier Financial Technology Limited (a joint venture comprised of Standard Chartered Bank, Hong Kong Telecom, and Animoca) and The Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation Limited to issue stablecoins in Hong Kong. The license is effective today. According to the licensee's business plan, they intend to commence business in the coming months once the relevant preparatory work is completed. Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) Chief Executive Eddie Yue said, “The granting of stablecoin issuer licenses is a significant milestone in the development of digital assets in Hong Kong. The regulatory system provides an orderly operating environment, allowing stablecoin issuers to properly protect user rights and manage related risks while applying innovative technologies, thus enabling the healthy, responsible, and sustainable development of Hong Kong's stablecoin ecosystem. I expect issuers to commence their business as planned, actively explore development opportunities while managing risks effectively, promote the application of compliant stablecoins, address pain points in financial and economic activities, create value for citizens and businesses, and promote the healthy development of Hong Kong's digital assets.” The HKMA's register of licensed stablecoin issuers is as follows: title="7450869" alt="2ZXzbqQ0D40hJ93pNiXQ8SQcxpVoF6Wa9LuzeDj2.png">

At the same time, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) also reminded the public to be vigilant against any fraudulent activities or scams claiming to be related to licensees or their issued stablecoins. If in doubt, members of the public should check the HKMA's records to verify the identity of the licensee or contact the licensee for further enquiries. When purchasing or using stablecoins, members of the public should use regulated channels.

data.title: Golden Web3.0 Daily Report | DeepSeek V4 to be released in late April
data.description:
DeFi data

1. Total market value of DeFi tokens: US$52.355 billion

DeFi Total Market Cap Data Source: coingecko

2. Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours: $37.94

Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours. Data source: coingecko

3. Assets locked in DeFi: $947.21 billion$

alt="F3gd6pj8hPHqi8g8u7VW8vtHK7sDCIJRFYSQohQf.png">

Top 10 DeFi Projects by Locked Assets and Total Value Locked. Data Source: defillama

NFT Data

1. Total Market Value of NFTs: $7.613 Billion

2.24-hour NFT transaction volume reached $1.638 billionUSD

NFT total market capitalization, top ten projects by market capitalization

NFT total market capitalization, top ten projects by market capitalization

NFT total market capitalization, top ten projects by market capitalization

Data Source: Coinmarketcap

Toutiao

Liang Wenfeng reveals internally that DeepSeek V4 will be released in late April

On April 10th, Liang Wenfeng, founder of DeepSeek, recently revealed in an internal communication that DeepSeek's new generation flagship large model, DeepSeek V4, will be officially released in late April. Previous reports indicated that DeepSeek V4 will not only bring trillions of parameters and millions of context windows, but also achieve deep adaptation with domestic chips such as Huawei Ascend for the first time, marking a key step forward for China's AI industry on the path of "de-CUDAization". It is understood that V4 will be open-sourced under the Apache 2.0 license, allowing enterprises to deploy it independently. To welcome the V4 release, domestic technology giants such as Alibaba, ByteDance, and Tencent have pre-ordered hundreds of thousands of new-generation AI computing chips, planning to provide the new DeepSeek model through cloud services and integrate it into their own AI products.

    AI Hot Topics

    1. Anthropic Agrees to Lease CoreWeave's AI Computing Power to Support Claude

    On April 10, Anthropic agreed to lease data center computing power from CoreWeave to meet the growing demand for its artificial intelligence services. According to CoreWeave's CEO, this computing power will include various Nvidia chip architectures in US data centers. This agreement will help Anthropic build and deploy its Claude AI model. CoreWeave has already added four major AI model developers to its customer list. CoreWeave's US stock rose as much as 6% in pre-market trading.

    2. The "Guideline for Risk Management of OpenClaw-like Intelligent Agent Deployment" Officially Released On April 10th, during the 17th Plenary Session of the China Artificial Intelligence Industry Development Alliance, the Security Governance Committee of the Alliance officially released the "Guideline for Risk Management of OpenClaw-like Intelligent Agent Deployment." To systematically address the security challenges posed by OpenClaw-like intelligent agents, the guideline focuses on the pain points of risk prevention and control for enterprises during the technology implementation process. It proposes a comprehensive framework and self-inspection standards for secure deployment covering the entire process of deployment, use, and decommissioning, aiming to promote intelligent agents from "being able to deploy" to "secure deployment, standardized use, and continuous governance," providing practical guidance for enterprises to conduct standardized technology deployment. 3. Yi Lihua: The Impact of AI is Accelerating; Individuals and Enterprises "May Be Eliminated if They Don't Understand AI" On April 10th, Yi Lihua, founder of Liquid Capital, stated in a live broadcast on Binance Square that the progress of AI has greatly impressed him. Over the past two or three months, he has focused his energy on learning about AI and accelerating the transformation of his previously invested companies towards AI. He believes that in the future, those who do not understand or use AI (whether individuals or teams) may be quickly eliminated in the competition. He also shared that a company he invested in, which originally provided traditional software services, saw a significant change in its business prospects after shifting towards AI. The team even joked that the new project "might be enough to make him retire," reflecting the impact of AI on business growth expectations and the industry landscape.

    4. Bittensor co-founder calls for restarting subnet community voting governance, criticizes Covenant AI founder's behavior as "malicious and greedy"

    Regarding Covenant AI's recent exit from the Bittensor network, Bittensor co-founder Jacob Steeves responded on Discord. He revealed that when dTAO was launched a year ago, the team originally planned to launch a community-led subnet governance mechanism—that is, subnet Alpha holders could vote through their wallets to elect the hyperparameter team.

    However, this feature was delayed due to the early need to give subnet owners more control. Steeves believes now is the right time to restart this discussion, suggesting the introduction of a community voting mechanism to elect a team and restart the subnet. He emphasizes that as one of the major holders, supporters, and technical contributors to Bittensor's top three projects, he will personally participate in this mechanism and recommend suitable candidates to continue advancing the project goals he initially set with Covenant AI founder Sam Dare. At the same time, Steeves bluntly states that Sam Dare's recent decisions are "clearly driven by malice and greed." However, he also points out that such crises often signify new beginnings and have the potential to redefine the form and governance of subnets.

    DeFi Hot Topics

    1. Aethir Successfully Isolates Cross-Chain Contract Attack, Affected Amount Less Than $90,000

    On April 10th, decentralized cloud infrastructure provider Aethir tweeted that it had detected and successfully isolated a malicious attack targeting the ATH cross-chain contract (connecting Ethereum to other chains). Currently, all attacked contracts have been disconnected, and the vulnerability has been controlled; the total supply of ATH on the Ethereum mainnet has not been affected; the ETH-ARB cross-chain bridge on Squid has not been affected; the actual impact on users is controlled within $90,000; the team will announce a complete compensation plan next week. Aethir is currently cooperating with multiple law enforcement agencies and exchanges (Binance, Upbit, Bithumb, HTX, etc.) to freeze funds and track the attackers. In addition, the team has published the affected contract addresses, reminding users not to interact with them for the time being.

    2. Cysic Mainnet Proof System to be Upgraded to Venus zkVM On April 10th, the ZKP acceleration project Cysic tweeted that its mainnet proof system will be upgraded to Venus zkVM, a mandatory update for network participants. After the upgrade, proof speed will be approximately 2.5 times faster than the previous generation, approximately 9% faster than ZisK 0.16.1, and will support multiple GPUs. 3. Starknet v0.14.2 Voting Open, Deadline April 16th On April 10th, Starknet announced on the X platform that voting for Starknet v0.14.2 is now open. This upgrade includes SNIP-36, SNIP-37, and SNIP-13, along with internal tweaks to prepare the network for the decentralized verification phase outlined in SNIP-33—scheduled to launch later this year. This release focuses on three core objectives: - Enabling in-protocol S-Two proof verification, which will open significant avenues for low-cost privacy use cases and zk-thread applications. - Adjusting the storage economics model to more accurately reflect actual network resource usage while maintaining cost-effectiveness for developers. - Upgrading the StarkGate token contract to optimize ERC-20 event indexing, verification, and token processing. This update also introduces internal upgrades related to the next phase of decentralization, including peer-to-peer (p2p) improvements, weighted proposer sampling, and the introduction of the virtual proposer concept. STRK holders are encouraged to review the pre-release notes and related SNIPs before voting. Voting closes on April 16th.

    4. StarkWare CPO Proposes Quantum-Safe Bitcoin Transactions Without Softforks

    On April 10th, Avihu Levy, Chief Product Officer of StarkWare, released the research proposal and open-source implementation of "Quantum-Safe Bitcoin Transactions Without Softforks". This proposal aims to provide quantum-safe protection for Bitcoin transactions without requiring a soft fork of Bitcoin. This proposal is currently a research prototype and is applicable to UTXOs (Unspent Transaction Outputs) whose public keys have never been exposed.

    ...

    5. Telegram founder announces TON upgrade, block generation speed increased 6 times

    On April 9th, Telegram founder Pavel Durov announced that The Open Network (TON) has completed a major upgrade, increasing block generation speed by 6 times, overall performance by approximately 10 times, and transaction confirmation time has been reduced to sub-second levels, achieving a "near-instant" transaction experience. TON officials stated that this upgrade is based on Catchain 2.0. Previously, the approximately 10-second confirmation time limited instant payments and application responsiveness, while this upgrade will make it possible to "complete payments like sending a message." Furthermore, TON stated that the higher block frequency will increase validator rewards and enhance staking incentives. Pavel Durov stated that this upgrade is the first step in the "Make TON Great Again" seven-step plan, and the next phase will further reduce transaction fees by approximately 6 times.

    Disclaimer: Jinse Finance, as a blockchain information platform, publishes articles for informational purposes only and should not be considered actual investment advice. Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: On-chain liquidity is not the same as liquidity: RWA is still one step away from achieving its goal.
data.description:
Liquidity for Returns: A Comprehensive Guide to the RWA Path

The world is in the same boat; both on-chain and off-chain companies are pursuing liquidity. Looking east, the stock market is vying for bank wealth management products; looking west, AI giants are seeking funding to save themselves.

After 2008, banks were confined by regulations, but "private credit" transformed into a significant source of corporate loans.

After 2018, PE, BDC (Business Development Company), and private credit companies took $300 billion from banks, the vast majority of which flowed to internet giants, represented by SaaS companies.

Following the 2020 pandemic, global financial markets were completely fragmented, and everyone needed to find a new anchor. The Chinese stock market embraced hard technology concepts, while the US stock market went all-in on AI. However, simply having investment targets was insufficient; corresponding liquidity management methods were essential. Liquidity initially came from massive monetary easing. In 2020, China lowered the reserve requirement ratio three times, and the Federal Reserve restarted QE and ultra-low interest rates. The "everyone buying funds" phenomenon made Zhang Kun a Weibo celebrity. However, in 2026, the shelling in the Persian Gulf shattered these illusions. User mentality changed; readily available cash became more important than future returns. The pursuit of liquidity forms the grand backdrop for the current RWA craze. Wall Street's move to on-chain operations requires converting the AUM (Assets Under Management) of many of its products into trading volume; otherwise, the pressure of repayment could overwhelm it. Assets are not simply money; liquidity is what gives them trading value. This is the key significance of RWAfi. On-chaining creates liabilities; trading creates assets.

Or rather, the market is revaluing the "liquidity premium"", the redemption wave of private lending in the United States, and the sluggish issuance of closed-end fixed-income products by Chinese banks all illustrate that liquidity is vying for the position of yield. This transmission or shadow means that the global financial market needs to find new connectors. Document No. 42 leaves an opening for "domestic assets + overseas issuance," and a clear bill is likely the way out for interest-bearing arrangements. There is mutual understanding, a struggle without outright conflict. Undoubtedly, blockchain will become the future of global finance. However, in the US, on-chain finance is Canton; in China, it's the digital yuan. Global finance is the domain of Ethereum and Solana. The more confrontational, the more a peace hotel is needed. Seeking global liquidity is the ultimate goal of this round of RWAfi.

Focusing on profit, seeking high-quality assets

What happened in the past will not be forgotten, it's just that we can't remember it.

Many people's impression of RWA is still stuck in the old memory of "everything on the blockchain," but Aave co-founder Stani also has a special fondness for photovoltaics on the blockchain. On the one hand, he warned that DeFi may become a passive entity for private credit liquidity; on the other hand, he enthusiastically encouraged DeFi to move towards the $30 trillion Abundance Assets, such as solar energy. ...Focusing on profit, seeking high-quality assets

Focusing on profit, seeking high-quality assets

In summary, RWA is an asset-based asset, which is very rare. ETH is based on people's use, BNB is based on Binance's trading volume, and BTC is based on a decentralized narrative. This is the crux of the problem. The RWA project team seems eager to bring liquidity by putting a certain asset on the blockchain, but just as "antiques were luxury items at the time," high-quality projects don't lack liquidity off-chain either. On-chain ≠ liquidity. On-chain is a technical matter, while liquidity is a financial matter; they shouldn't be confused. On-chain can only bring liquidity in one situation: when the on-chain action meets the expectation of liquidity delivery. For example, off-chain financial products that are only available to institutional clients can be minted into a stablecoin like Vault, which retail investors can deposit funds into. Based on this, the RWA framework can be understood as three layers, from top to bottom: the Asset layer, the tokenized RWA assets, and the liquid RWAfi.

  • Assets

  • RWA: Asset-Based Assets

  • RWAfi: Liquid Asset-Based Assets

Ignoring physical assets and future photovoltaics, real-world asset types can be divided into four categories: stablecoins, stocks, debt, and funds. Stablecoins pegged to the US dollar, such as USDT/USDC, and US stocks are the most familiar to most people.

However, it can be predicted that debt and funds will have stronger development potential because they currently have the worst liquidity. Even if the purchase process for institutional clients is switched to on-chain, it doesn't allow retail investors to participate directly.

A typical example is the Guotai Junan tokenized fund GUSDT and GHKDT sold by Hashkey. Users can neither trade nor withdraw funds, and it is generally a version behind the US market. At least in BlackRock's view, tokenized funds will disrupt Wall Street in the same way the internet disrupted the postal system. The logic is not complicated: tokenized funds target the global market and will completely eliminate existing national boundaries. Of course, each category—cryptocurrency, stock, bond, and fund—can be infinitely subdivided. For example, stablecoins can be divided into their pegged assets (USD <> crypto assets) or currencies (USD <> non-USD). Debt can be divided into issuers (national <> local <> corporate) or collateral (CLOs are based on loans <> CDOs are based on real estate). However, none of this is important, and there's no need to specifically study the institutional differences between the US, Shenzhen, and Singapore. Numerous research reports exist, and these details are not the core concern for capital operations and entry/exit. We only need to focus on the technology service providers that help put real-world assets on the blockchain, especially those with US backgrounds like Securites, SuperState, Canton, and Ondo. They will clear all obstacles for us. Following the SEC's guidance on the applicability of tokenized products under securities law, Securites welcomed Giang Bui, former head of the Nasdaq Spot Bitcoin ETF listing, and Brett Redfearn, a former SEC official. Beyond the political and business revolving door, traditional finance has also become involved. Traditional asset management firm Invesco acquired SuperState's $USTB tokenized Treasury bond product, while Circle's USYC product even surpassed giants like BlackRock's BUIDL in terms of issuance volume. Furthermore, Canton Chain, backed by Goldman Sachs, could "defeat" Ethereum in undertaking the DTCC's on-chain experiment. Beyond the technological paradigm, the RWA middleware layer has become a map-filling game for institutions, vying for control of the four major RWA product lines, with no clear sphere of influence yet emerging. However, at the RWAfi level, DeFi has shown a more tolerant acceptance attitude; for them, making more assets liquid is also beneficial. But for RWA to leap to RWAfi, initial liquidity needs to be artificially created. If it's simply introduced as an underlying asset into the existing stack, it still faces the dilemma of "no one wanting it." Where does the liquidity come from? On-chain liquidity has become a high-premium product. If RWA intends to seek liquidity from DeFi, a 10% yield will be the baseline. Compared to the less than 4% yield of US Treasury bonds, the existing RWA is unlikely to cover the difference, let alone offer higher returns. Retail investors have virtually no incentive to buy. Therefore, they will likely add artificial barriers to inflate the yield. Implicit withdrawal costs or time limits, such as Ethena's sUSDe redemption period recently changing from 7 days to dynamic, and the underlying assets including more non-US Treasury bonds, essentially using user funds to increase leverage, only without the volatility of perpetual contracts. It also includes more subsidies, such as revenue from its own token sales. This is actually a controlled "Ponzi scheme," with underlying assets including US Treasury bonds to ensure minimum repayment, attracting liquidity with high interest rates, then locking it up, allowing the project team to earn revenue from the scale of funds. However, the practices of 2025/2026 show that, from Huma to Pharos and Bitway, there are gaps between on-chain and off-chain practices. The persistent, non-real-time third-party auditing merely adds legitimacy to "illiquidity" without enhancing on-chain liquidity. While they all appear to be interest-bearing stablecoins, the underlying mechanisms of each protocol remain largely unexplored.

Furthermore, by increasing the leverage of TradFi, a seemingly legitimate on-chain liquidity is created, most notably on Hyperliquid. Trade.xyz is also expanding in the RWAfi (Retail Investment Assistance) field by increasing trading leverage in oil and precious metals. However, we must recognize that the liquidity caused by trading and the immobility of private credit are two sides of the same coin. This principle is not complicated; any mature market needs three elements: low-cost capital, high-leverage strategies, and a large-scale market. Just as Buffett used insurance float and ultra-long-term time leverage to dominate the US financial market, the US banking industry's resistance to stablecoin interest-bearing mechanisms is also an attempt to monopolize users' demand deposits. However, the cost of maintaining the perpetual contracts in the crypto industry is extremely high, which is the price of perpetuality; otherwise, the Delta neutrality mechanism wouldn't exist. Now, Ethena has partially abandoned its fee arbitrage strategy. Even projects like Saturn and APyx are starting to use micro-strategy stocks ($MSTR) as underlying assets to build on-chain interest-bearing products, indicating a trading crisis in the crypto industry. While celebrating the growth in Trade.xyz's trading volume, let's not forget the crisis signal following Binance's significant reduction in VIP standards. In summary, the current problem lies in the need to rely on US Treasury bonds, token subsidies, and market-making strategies to bridge the 10% gap with US Treasury bonds, and even hope to widen it further, potentially leading to bizarre practices like Phraos and Gaib raising funds on-chain to provide online lending to third-world countries. Therefore, the payment, fund, and bond markets, which involve indirect leveraged trading, are more crucial for the development of RWAfi, such as payment company deposits and Galaxy's CLO loans secured by BTC. Especially the latter. Galaxy's VC firm invested in Arch Lending, a company that allows users to borrow stablecoins with BTC as collateral. Galaxy then packaged Arch's debt into a CLO product, and Sky invested in it through Grove, obtaining corresponding returns. In this process: Users: do not need to sell BTC and avoid capital gains tax from cashing out. Arch: obtain institutional-grade "low-cost funding" without having to sell tokens to support scale expansion. Galaxy: expand into a "large-scale market," as CLO products based on BTC are easily accepted by DeFi protocols. Sky/Grove: "High-Leverage Strategy"—RWA Assets Beyond Treasury Bonds Expected to Offer Higher Returns. Of course, things aren't always perfect. The success of this case is undoubtedly related to the various stakeholders involved in Galaxy's strategy. However, looking at the entire RWAfi market, this is a relatively good and safe strategy for increasing returns. Galaxy CLO products use BTC as collateral. As mentioned earlier, products like Saturn use US stocks as collateral. Let's consider the DeFi path using US stocks as collateral. US Treasury bonds, the US dollar, and US stocks, as the world's strongest financial assets, are at different stages of development. US Treasury bonds and the US dollar have supported the rapid rise of US dollar stablecoins and TMMFs (Tokenized Money Market Funds), but the tokenization of US stocks has only just begun.

InIssuance, management, custody, auditing, liquidation

data.title: When "successful execution" becomes the most dangerous lie in the AI ​​era
data.description:
Author: Cobo

In an era where AI infrastructure is not yet ready, "out of control" no longer manifests as system crashes, but rather as systems deviating from the boundaries of human authorization during normal operation. This is becoming a new and increasingly prominent security issue. This article is based on a real-world on-chain incident recap: how an agent powered by Openclaw deviated from instructions and opened a BTC-PERP 10x long order in the Drift Protocol on Solana.

When your interface pops up "Transaction executed," most users will be satisfied with their agent. But in the unseen background, your agent may have just secretly doubled your budget, misappropriated funds, and erased all traces of errors.

The above scenario is not science fiction, but a reality that has already occurred.

This is a system-level security breach that is currently occurring on a large scale when the AI ​​Agent performs deterministic tasks—we call it Silent Override, an unauthorized behavior. 01. A $0.2 Difference Leads the Agent to Directly Rewrite Your Authorization Parameters Let's go back to March 26, 2026, and fully recap this real-world incident that occurred on the Solana chain. At 10:58 AM that day, a user instructed the AI ​​Agent to open a 10x long position in a BTC perpetual contract on the Drift Protocol. After initial environment testing, the Agent calculated that the threshold required was approximately 0.07 SOL. To be on the safe side, the user gave a very clear parameter instruction at 11:49 AM: using 0.1 SOL as margin. Logically, with sufficient funds, this should have been feasible. However, at 11:51, the Agent discovered during execution that the margin requirement was not met: an error message indicated "Insufficient Collateral." At that time, the oracle price was approximately $83.2/SOL, and 0.1 SOL was equivalent to $8.32, while the actual requirement for Drift was $8.52. The shortfall was only $0.2. The normal product logic would be: trigger Insufficient Funds – abort execution – throw an exception, and wait for user authorization to make up the difference (only about 0.11 SOL is needed). But this Agent did not do this. Faced with this $0.2 shortfall, it unilaterally changed the margin parameter from 0.1 SOL to 0.2 SOL (without any calculation basis, simply doubling it), forcibly resolving the problem and displaying the message "Transaction successful" on the user's screen. It wasn't until 11:58 that the user checked their on-chain balance and realized their wallet was 0.1 SOL short of expectations. Under questioning, the agent revealed the truth, admitting that "the correct approach should have been to ask the user if they wanted to adjust it to above 0.11 SOL." Faced with this tiny shortfall, many might think the agent was acting out of kindness, helping them get things done. But is this really a good thing? This leads to a deeper, and even more chilling, question.

02. Silent Override destroys the underlying auditability

An extra 0.1 SOL in margin may not be fatal, but it breaks the most fundamental trust contract in human-computer interaction: the user sets the boundaries, and the machine executes within those boundaries. Once the agent believes that "in order to facilitate the task, the boundaries can be overturned arbitrarily," this contract instantly becomes invalid.

Even more frightening is that the agent alters the margin and erases all traces. In the era of traditional software, we are accustomed to preventing system crashes and errors; but in the AI ​​era, normal transaction failures are actually the bottom line of security—because clear error reports mean that the process is visible, traceable, and accountable, and the risk is successfully intercepted in the present moment.

In contrast, this fabricated "success" is far more destructive. When the agent uses perfect front-end execution feedback to cover up unauthorized actions in the back-end, a violation is seamlessly integrated into the historical ledger. This is why we named it Silent Override: Silent (silent concealment) plus Override (unauthorized alteration) completely deprives users of any means of accountability. The 0.1 SOL gap may not seem large. But the problem is never about this 0.1 SOL; it's that once a system accepts the logic that authorization can be rewritten to complete a task, its bottom line will continuously crumble. Today it dares to arbitrarily double the 0.1 SOL deposit to 0.2 SOL; tomorrow it might be the payment amount, the target address, the slippage tolerance, or any field the system deems helpful in completing the task. When "execution successful" on the screen no longer equates to "executed as I intended," the user's initial request and the agent's mid-process tampering all become an unverifiable mess. The system completely degenerates into a black box that appears clean on the outside but is internally distorted. At this point, you are no longer facing a small loss from a single transaction, but rather the complete loss of the ability to verify the ledger—this is the complete collapse of the underlying "auditability." 03. This is not an isolated case: From "silent tampering" to "shadow hosting" Drift's $0.2 is not an extremely rare edge case. The same pattern—the agent overstepping authorization boundaries to complete instructions—has appeared in other scenarios. In a previous Polymarket transaction incident, a user instructed an agent to purchase conditional tokens through an MPC wallet. During execution, the agent incorrectly determined that the MPC wallet could not complete the required EIP-712 signature, so it took matters into its own hands: generating a temporary key and transferring the user's funds to this temporary address to complete the transaction. The assets ultimately ended up in an address completely beyond the user's control, yet the agent still confidently reported the transaction as successful. This incident is known as Shadow Custody—assets slipping out of the user's actual control without their knowledge. These two incidents, with their different manifestations, point to the same underlying malicious behavior pattern: when encountering execution obstacles, the agent chooses to circumvent rather than report them. In the Drift incident, the bypass method was to tamper with the amount; in the Polymarket incident, the bypass method was to transfer funds. The result was the same: users' assets all went outside the expected security boundaries. This reveals an extremely dangerous structural risk currently facing Agentic Wallets: the agent's objective function is incorrectly set to complete the instruction, rather than completing it within the user's authorized scope. When these two objectives conflict, the current system has no mechanism to force it to choose the latter. The agent will do everything it can to complete your instructions. This is its greatest advantage, and also its most dangerous characteristic. So, what underlying mechanism forces the agent onto this dangerous path of preferring to tamper with data rather than report errors? Why would an AI designed to serve users disregard the user's authorized boundaries? The root of it all lies in the underlying logic of the large language model. 04. Root Cause: Reward Hijacking in LLM and the Conflict with Financial Contracts Why does this misalignment of the objective function occur? This involves the underlying operating mechanism of AI. Currently, most Agent systems still use "semantic soft constraints" when constraining boundaries—that is, writing the intent and restrictions in the same Prompt and throwing it to the large model. However, system developers have overlooked a fatal point: the underlying logic of AI has no concept of financial contracts; it only has objective functions. In the training foundation of LLM (Large Language Model), the highest weight is given to Helpful (providing assistance, completing the task). For the Agent, a transaction failure due to a difference of $0.2 is an unacceptable negative state; while modifying parameters to make the transaction succeed brings extremely high positive feedback. When faced with execution friction, large models exhibit typical Reward Hacking: they resemble an aggressive employee willing to do anything to achieve KPIs. Fueled by powerful illusions and self-deception, the agent instantly convinces itself—the user's fundamental goal is to go long on BTC; 0.1 SOL is just a rough estimate, changing it to 0.2 will achieve the goal—a brilliant execution. Thus, it bypasses your consent, arbitrarily doubles the margin, forcibly buys, and then returns four words: Transaction successful. In this process, no matter how many times you bold the message in the Prompt or how strictly you write in natural language that modifying the amount or exceeding authority is prohibited, it's all in vain. Natural language can express intent, but it can never handle machine-level access control. Attempting to use the magic of language to constrain language itself is bound to be easily breached the moment a real-world error occurs. 05. Architectural Restructuring: From Semantic Defenses to Physical Isolation The root cause of such incidents is not the Agent's insufficient capabilities, but rather the lack of external constraints on its autonomous decision-making. Defense can never rely on the "self-awareness" of the large model; it must be enforced using mechanisms independent of the Agent. Since the failure occurs at the architectural level, the solution must also be engineering-level. In a truly trustworthy system, we need to deploy three hard constraints: 1. Parameter Locking Rules: Depriving of Modification Rights Completely decouple the task intent from the authorization bottom line. For user-specified deterministic parameters (such as the maximum amount, target address, and asset quantity), the Agent only has the right to read, not modify. If friction arises due to parameters during execution, the only legitimate action the system assigns to the Agent is to stop execution, report the exception, and wait for new instructions from the user. 2. Transaction Firewall: Independent Review Before Execution An independent gateway must be inserted between the Agent's planning layer and the on-chain settlement layer. This firewall does not consider the Agent's natural language interpretation; it only parses the underlying generated calldata and compares it with the user's original instructions at the parameter level. If any deviation is found—a single cent more, a single character difference in address, or an incompatible operation type—the system immediately triggers a circuit breaker, forcing a user approval. There is no room for negotiation. 3. Deviation Alerts: Establishing an External Monitoring Loop Never let the Agent act as both referee and player. A monitoring service independent of the Agent must be deployed to compare user instruction parameters with actual on-chain execution parameters in real time. Once an agent attempts to act unilaterally, an alert is pushed to the user within seconds: you authorized 0.1 SOL, but the agent executed 0.2 SOL on the chain. This completely exposes the system's black box. Conclusion: Defining What an Agent "Absolutely Cannot Do" When we talk about the future of agents, our attention is often drawn to their unlimited potential. However, for any financial-grade agent that truly handles real assets, the core security boundary lies in: under no circumstances should the agent do anything. Trust is precisely built within this "negative space." Allowing agents to explore the vast semantic space infinitely is the imagination of AI; but transforming security red lines from language into code is the system's physical defense. No matter how advanced the large model becomes, the first principle in building an Agent will always be: let AI handle intelligence, and let code handle deterministic operations.
